problem.title: Subsets
problem.description: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set).
assumptions: Return power set (all subsets including empty and full set).

example.explanation: All 2ⁿ subsets. Backtracking: include or exclude each element. Or bit manipulation.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Backtracking [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Backtracking explores every choice of including or skipping each element from a start index onward, recording a copy of the current path at every recursive call — not just at leaves — because every prefix is a valid subset. This avoids duplicates by always advancing the start index forward. The key insight is that each of the 2^n subsets corresponds to exactly one path through this decision tree.
solutions.code_python:
from typing import List

def subsets(nums: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
    result: List[List[int]] = []

    def backtrack(start: int, current: List[int]) -> None:
        result.append(list(current))
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1, current)
            current.pop()

    backtrack(0, [])
    return result

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Iterative Bit Manipulation [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: For an array of n elements there are exactly 2^n subsets. For each integer i from 0 to 2^n - 1, treat each bit of i as a flag: if bit j is set, include nums[j] in the current subset. This generates all subsets without recursion. O(n * 2^n) time O(n * 2^n) space.
